issues.title: Easy way to enable/disable endpoint
issues.body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

This is not a problem but rather something that occurs quite commonly. Sometimes you just want to expose a certain endpoint based on configuration settings or environment. Currently there is `include_in_schema` flag that hides the endpoint in OpenAPI schema but the actual endpoint is still present and responsive. There should be a way to disable endpoint entirely from being exposed.


**Describe the solution you'd like**

I think there should be a `disabled` or `enabled` flag on `APIRoute.api_route` method to easily enable the endpoint and not exposing it at all.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
I do this at the router level, and conditionally call `include_router` depending on the value of a config variable.

It looks like this:

```python
router = APIRouter()
admin_router = APIRouter()

... # add some endpoints here

def get_router() -> APIRouter:
    combined_router = deepcopy(router)
    if get_api_settings().include_admin_routes:
        combined_router.include_router(admin_router, dependencies=require_superuser())
    return combined_router
```

comments.author: LKay
comments.body: This works for the whole router but disabling single endpoint inside a router is different case. Let’s say I have a router with 4 endpoints but I want to disable only one of them based on a flag.

comments.author: euri10
dmontagu
LKay
comments.body:
I see my case is yet slightly different. I have one function with logic implemented and it’s decorated by more than one route decorator.

This is to handle case such as `/users/:id/notes` and `/notes` having the same notes fetching logic but `id` parameter from path if present overrides the user id from authorization token. I would like to be able to disable certain decorators based on some condition.

This is not an actual code but I think it illustrates the case.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
You could still implement that by just putting the decorator statement (`f = app.route(...)(f)`) inside a conditional.

I know it would be nicer to keep the decorator above the function, but this seems like a pretty uncommon edge case — my suspicion is that if this were added, it would get very little use.

I could be convinced otherwise if other people want the feature though.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: Also, for what it’s worth, it might be worth using a query parameter instead of a path parameter for your example. That would eliminate the issue entirely (not to mention simplify the api spec) — you’d be able to handle the condition within the endpoint.

comments.author: LKay
comments.body: Handled using wrapping `if` statements around routes.

issues.title: OAuth2 Authorization Code flow fails
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**

I believe the following code should implement the OAuth2 Authorization Code flow for the openapi/swagger docs interface:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends
from fastapi.openapi.models import OAuthFlows as OAuthFlowsModel
from fastapi.security import OAuth2


CLIENT_ID = "MYVERYSECRETID"
CLIENT_SECRET = "MYVERYSSECRETSECRET"
API_BASE_URL = "https://my.oauth2.server.com"
AUTHORIZE_URL = f"{API_BASE_URL}/as/authorization.oauth2"
ACCESS_TOKEN_URL = f"{API_BASE_URL}/as/token.oauth2"
SCOPES = {"profile": "profile", "openid": "openid"}


app = FastAPI()  # pylint: disable=invalid-name


auth_code_flow = OAuthFlowsModel(
    authorizationCode={
        "tokenUrl": ACCESS_TOKEN_URL,
        "authorizationUrl": AUTHORIZE_URL,
        "scopes": SCOPES,
    }
)

oauth2_scheme = OAuth2(flows=auth_code_flow)


@app.post("/test")
async def login_for_access_token(token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)):
    print("in /test")

```

This successfully produces an Authorization button in the `/docs` swagger UI, which when used successfuly takes the user to the authorization service to enter credentials. 

The next part of the process, exchanging the authorization code for a token, which should happen server side not client side, fails. In the UI I see `Auth ErrorTypeError: Failed to fetch`. Looking at the network traffic I see that the *client* is trying to connect to the TOKEN_URL, and (obviously) failing. My understanding is that this is not how Authorization Code Flow is supposed to work - the authorization code should be passed from the client to the API server, and the API server should take care of exchanging the code for a token by going to the token URL.

In the console where I am running the fastAPI application I see:

```
INFO: ('127.0.0.1', 56658) - "GET /docs HTTP/1.1" 200
INFO: ('127.0.0.1', 56658) - "GET /openapi.json HTTP/1.1" 200
INFO: ('127.0.0.1', 56665) - "GET /docs/oauth2-redirect?code=y2Kw0z-EYXXXX2jAAAAAw&state=VGh1IFNlcCAxOSAyMDE5IDEwOjMyOjUXXXXNVCswMTAwIChCcml0aXNoIFN1bW1lciBUaW1lKQ%3D%3D HTTP/1.1" 200
```
(Have X'ed out some characters)

This is the problem I referred to in #335


**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. See above.

**Expected behavior**
I would expect to see successful authentication.

**Screenshots**

**Environment:**
 fastapi 0.38.1


- Python version, get it with:
Python 3.7.4

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
~Based on reading [these docs](https://auth0.com/docs/flows/concepts/auth-code) it seems like it is behaving correctly:~

> 6. Auth0's SDK sends this code to the Auth0 Authorization Server (/oauth/token endpoint) along with the application's Client ID and Client Secret.

~My interpretation is that the "Auth0 SDK" is basically equivalent to your client, and so it sounds to me like they are saying the client should be the thing sending the code to the `/token` endpoint. (For reference, the "Auth0 Tenant" is basically equivalent to your auth server.)~

Re-reading the docs, I'm not sure if the above interpretation is right any more.

comments.author: jonathanunderwood
comments.body:
> I think the above interpretation might be wrong.

Yup - in that picture, the "Regular web app" is both the client and the API server. It's a really confusing picture that had me scratching my head for a couple of hours a few weeks back. Glad to see I'm not the only one that finds it a bit confusing.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: For what it's worth, the swagger docs client logic is handled entirely outside of FastAPI (FastAPI just includes a reference to a cdn-hosted script); I think [this repo](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui) might be a better place to look for information (or post an issue).

comments.author: gregorybchris
comments.body: To fix `Auth Error Error: Not Found` I had to ensure that the login endpoint was named `/token`. Naming the endpoint `/login` or something similar will not work as the Swagger UI looks specifically for `/token`.

comments.author: asionesjia
comments.body:
Sorry, this is the machine translated version. I have successfully solved this problem in my program. The main reason for this problem is that the address pointed to by the Swagger authorization button is not the same as your login api address. Just change the tokenUrl to the correct path. If not, it may not be the same as your problem.
`oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="/users/login")`

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/88858759/196038744-4aa3f6a1-c1c3-4faa-b07f-3a83107b727a.png)

issues.title: Issue when debugging inside docker
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**
When I run pdb in docker I run into issues if I have reload enabled.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.requests import Request

import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from app.api.api import api_router
from app.core import config
from app.db.session import Session


app = FastAPI(title='App')
app.include_router(api_router, prefix=config.API_V1_STR)


@app.middleware("http")
async def db_session_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
   
    # This set_trace crashes only if I started uvicorn with `debug=True`
    import pdb; pdb.set_trace()
   
    request.state.db = Session()
    response = await call_next(request)
    request.state.db.close()
    return response


# For debugging
if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run("main:app", host="0.0.0.0", port=80, reload=True, debug=True)
```

**Expected behavior**
I should be able to use pdb, but instead get an `bdb.BdbQuit` exception. This doesn't happen if I run outside docker or if I run inside docker but remove `reload=True` argument.

**Environment:**
`FROM tiangolo/uvicorn-gunicorn:python3.7`

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
I have no idea what could be causing this, but my guess is that it's more likely to be a uvicorn issue than a fastapi issue. I'd recommend trying with a non-fastapi (and ideally non-starlette-based) framework -- if you have the same problem hitting breakpoints, it would indicate the problem is in uvicorn.

@euri10 any other suggestions for debugging in docker? (If I recall you have some experience with this.)

comments.author: euri10
comments.body:
I'm using pycharm which uses pydevd iirc so no experience with pdb.
My guess is that uvicorn reload uses another process and afaik you cant pbd in another process,hence the behavior with reload only. 
But you should ask in uvicorn imho, if a simple asgi app crashes the same.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: @euri10 that's a good insight -- I definitely think the separate process issue could explain the problem

comments.author: joaqo
comments.body: Hey, thanks for the answers! Separate processes could explain the issue, but its weird that reload + pdb works correctly outside docker, running it in macOS.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: Hmm that's a good point. Can you run it outside of docker on a machine with the same operating system as the container? (E.g., on a cloud instance?)

comments.author: joaqo
comments.body:
Found the bug! Tried it on a linux machine and it worked just fine. So I started reducing the error to the smallest code possible and it turns out that using `"main:app"` and `reload=True` in:

```python
uvicorn.run("main:app", host="0.0.0.0", port=80, reload=True, debug=True)
```
is what triggers the issue. If I just use `app` and `reload=True` it works fine. Thanks for the help.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: Weird! Glad you were able to figure it out! 🚀

comments.author: joaqo
dmontagu
comments.body:
I am not able to reproduce this -- your script seems to work fine for me in my own `backend-tests` docker container built on top of the official `python:3.7` image (which I believe is the base image used by `tiangolo/uvicon-gunicorn:python3.7`).

In particular, if I start the server, then run `curl 0.0.0.0` from another shell in the same container, the debugger starts as usual. Then, if I enter the command `c` when the debugger comes up, everything goes through as expected. If I enter the command `q` I get the BdbQuit error, but I get that same error in both macos and in the docker container (and I don't *think* that is how you are supposed to continue code execution anyway, but I'm not really a pdb user).

Are you doing something specific besides the above that is triggering the error?

comments.author: joaqo
comments.body:
I'm sorry, in the script I posted I used `app` instead of `'script_file_name:app'` in the uvicorn.run command. So if you just pasted that you didn't have reload enabled. If you use `'script_file_name:app'` you should get the same error I got. Sorry for wasting your time. This is the script that gives me an error while using Docker:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.requests import Request

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/")
def read_root():
    return {"Hello": "World"}


@app.middleware("http")
async def db_session_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
    import pdb; pdb.set_trace()
    return await call_next(request)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run('script_file_name:app', host="0.0.0.0", port=80, reload=True, debug=True)
```

To run it just do.
```bash
docker run -v /local_folder_with_script_above/:/test/ -p 80:80 tiangolo/uvicorn-gunicorn:python3.7
```
then run in another terminal:
```bash
docker exec -it {container_name} bash
```

and then inside the terminal just `pip install fastapi` and then `python /test/script_file_name.py`. Finally go to `localhost` in your browser. You should get an error in the above terminal instead of a pdb prompt.

issues.title: base href for swagger spec and ui
issues.body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Our fastapi service is place behind a proxy using path-based routing - i.e. /docs becames /foo/docs

This result in the default swagger spec as well as the swagger UI to fails as the openapi path passed to the html is an absolute ref (i.e. /docs or /prefix/docs) which will resolve incorrectly if there is a non-fastapi path routing.

See https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/fc7d123347591c0d79505ee0c6a3a5ace70192f1/fastapi/applications.py#L91

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Either incorporate a base href argument to fast API, or set the openapi_url to relative ref (i.e. ./docs or ./prefix/docs).

Similar fix should be done for the openapi spec generation. 

I would prefer the former solution as it will also correctly generate the openapi spec (to include the non-fastapi prefix).

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
Currently I provision my own custom route with the modified swagger UI HTML as well as the openapi spec.

issues.title: Do not log warning if email-validator not installed
issues.body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

I don't use email validation with pydantic in fastapi and I don't want to have my logs polluted with a warning about it.
This sentiment is also shared in https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/815

**Describe the solution you'd like**

There should be no warning logged.

I'm not clear if it's acceptable to just explode when email-validator is missing and an email field must be validated as suggested by https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/815.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Leaving it like that, it's not the end of the world to have this warning :)

comments.author: samuelcolvin
comments.body:
This check should be unnecessary in fastapi, pydantic will raise an import error when a model is initialised which includes an email field.

Surely they should be sufficient.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: @samuelcolvin except those email-using objects may be desirable to use (as part of your open api schema) even without validation. I think it makes sense to warn about the removal of validation, but it should be easier to suppress I think.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
I think something like this might be more appropriate; any thoughts?

```python
from enum import Enum
from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional, Union
import warnings

from pydantic import BaseModel, Schema as PSchema
from pydantic.types import UrlStr

try:
    import email_validator

    assert email_validator  # make autoflake ignore the unused import
    from pydantic.types import EmailStr  # type: ignore
except ImportError:  # pragma: no cover
    warnings.warn(
        "email-validator not installed, email fields will be treated as str.\n"
        "To install, run: pip install email-validator\n",
        "To suppress this warning, set environment variable "
        "PYTHONWARNINGS=ignore:::fastapi.openapi.models",
        ImportWarning
    )
    class EmailStr(str):  # type: ignore
        pass

...
```
(Not tested, so may need minor tweaks, but I think the general idea should work.)

comments.author: samuelcolvin
comments.body:
> even without validation

It's not validation that raises the `ImportError`, it's model initialisation. So even if no validation is called, the error will still occur. See [here](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/blob/master/pydantic/networks.py#L268).

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
Yes, sorry my point was just this: rather than adding email-validator as a dependency, I may just want to allow arbitrary strings as emails. But fastapi doesn’t want to do that without any warning to the user, which is why the warning exists.

If you know what is happening and are okay with it though, I think it should be possible to suppress the warning.

comments.author: cjw296
comments.body: @dmontagu - I think this creates enough noise and confusion that if there's a desire to use `EmailStr` without `email-validator`, then the application doing that should take care of catching the `ImportError` rather than forcing the (what I suspect is the) vast majority of users who don't use `EmailStr` and have to go out of their way to silence the warning.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
But it is used by fastapi internally -- is your point that you just don't want fastapi to warn you that it isn't going to validate email addresses if email-validator isn't installed?

I could imagine some edge cases where this results in confusing behavior (e.g., if you add email-validator as a dependency, then something breaks in your fastapi code because you were using an invalid value for an email address somewhere). But that should be so rare that I could get behind dropping the warning entirely -- it mostly just feels right to me to keep the warning "on principle", but practicality may dictate otherwise.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
For reference: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/55c4b5fb0bd260765b6eedbf8c200caa3cdc670e/fastapi/openapi/models.py#L16

https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/55c4b5fb0bd260765b6eedbf8c200caa3cdc670e/fastapi/openapi/models.py#L28

(The above line is the only place where EmailStr occurs.)

Given how inconsequential this is (a single optional field on a rarely used type) I feel like it's probably worth just dropping the warning.

comments.author: cjw296
comments.body: When are the things in models.py used? It doesn't seem like it's worth doing email validation then, so maybe just change that to be a `str` and drop the dependency on the package all together?

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
I went through the codebase and I only see one place where a reference to an `EmailStr` leaves `models.py`:

* `fastapi.models.Contact.email` is of type `Optional[EmailStr]`
* `fastapi.models.Info.contact` is of type `Optional[Contact]`
* `fastapi.models.OpenAPI.info` is of type `fastapi.models.Info`
* `fastapi.models.OpenAPI` is used at the end of `fastapi.openapi.utils.get_openapi`.

The thing is, it is currently not possible to set the `info` field of the generated `OpenAPI` to contain a non-`None` contact using`fastapi.openapi.utils.get_openapi`. So I suppose those models are just for convenience if you want to implement your own function to build the `OpenAPI` instance if overriding `fastapi.FastAPI.openapi()`.

Given all of this, I'd agree that I think it makes sense to  drop the dependency.

(Note: the `EmailStr` type is also currently used in some of the tests, and the tests do actually check that the generated schema says that the field has string format `'email'`. So either those tests would need to be changed, or `email-validator` would need to remain a development dependency. But I'm assuming we're fine with it as a development dependency.)

comments.author: cjw296
comments.body: `email-validator` as a test dependency seems sensible, but if we just turn that into a `str` field then those tests would need to change, right?

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: The tests don't check the schema of the openapi schema type -- they check the schema of an app which uses an unrelated model containing an EmailStr field. So those fields can remain as EmailStr. (There are no tests involving `fastapi.models.Contact`.)

comments.author: cjw296
comments.body: Ah, perfect!

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the conversation here everyone!

In a recent version, I added custom logic to log the warning only if the `EmailStr` is actually used, only if it receives a value: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/946

So, it should not show again, in most of the cases (only if you did custom stuff to include a contact in your OpenAPI).

That is available in latest version.

comments.body: I think this issue is good to close :-)

comments.body: yep, thanks to all!

issues.title: Ability to override the app-building in an APIRoute
issues.body:
I would like to be able to more easily override the use of `get_app` near the end of `APIRoute.__init__` so that I can inject some custom handling of the request, response, or the request-to-response pipeline without needing to replace the entire pipeline.

This would be useful, for example, in #521 in order to determine a response class as a function of a header without needing to replicate the code to do this in every endpoint.

-----

### More detail:
Thanks to the ability to specify a `route_class` when creating an `APIRouter`, it is now possible to override some of the behaviors in `APIRoute`. In particular, this provides a more targeted way to modify the request and/or response coming from a specific route as part of the `APIRoute` implementation.

However, one of the better targets for modification is the `app` property, which is created at the end of a rather long `__init__` method. Moreover, the stored property is the result of wrapping the result of `fastapi.routing.get_app` (which is readily modified) with `starlette.routing.request_response`, and the result of this function does not expose the result of the original `get_app` for modification. As a result, overriding the behavior of the `route_class` can involve repeating an undesirable amount of code from the base implementation.

(See #521 for an example of how this can be useful.)

-----

I see two routes to improving this:

1. Refactor the end of the `APIRoute.__init__` so that `get_app` is called inside a method:

    Currently:
    ```python
        ...
        self.app = request_response(get_app(...))
    ```
    Desired:
    ```python
        ...
        self.app = request_response(self.get_app(...))

        def get_app(...):
            return get_app(...)
    ```
    This approach would (with minimal changes) make it easier to modify the request and response from an endpoint by just overriding `APIRoute.get_app` (see #521 for an example).

2. Refactor the `fastapi.routing.get_app` function to make use of a *class* to build the `app`, rather than returning a 50-line function to use as the app. Some care would be required in order to preserve performance, but if the function returned by `get_app` could be built from a class that breaks out the various parts of the process into discrete steps, it would make it much easier to modify some of the steps. For example:
    * It would make it substantially easier to conditionally transform the request body prior to parsing. This would substantially simplify some of the workflows discussed in #200 and #381. It would also enable things like parsing exotic serialization formats prior to passing them to pydantic (as desired in #521).
    * It would make it much easier to have the response_class depend on the request parameters (e.g., the `Accept` header) -- handling this in the endpoint means lots of boilerplate for handling the request in each endpoint, but handling it in middleware would require both reloading the serialized json *and* converting the starlette StreamingResponse resulting from `starlette.routing.request_response` back to the desired response class (and possibly converting it back into a StreamingResponse again after that). The `get_app` function feels like the obvious place to handle this.

    This could also be combined with point 1. above, and would make it easy to override each component of the app by overriding the class used to build the app in the `get_app` function, and then overriding the `APIRoute.get_app` method to use the modified app building process, with minimal code.

issues.title: route_class not preserved when calling APIRouter.include_router
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**

When calling `APIRouter.include_router`, the class of the included routes should be preserved. Right now, this is not the case -- the `route_class` of the parent router is used. This means that the `route_class` is lost on inclusion if one isn't careful. Worse, it means that even if you manually create a router with multiple route-types present, there is no way to include that router in another without changing the route type of some of the routes.

See the `include_typed_router` function from #521 for an example of how this currently requires a workaround.

I have addressed this in the short PR #538, but wanted to create an issue for discussion in case there is any reason to preserve the current behavior.

comments.author: sandys
comments.body: is there an update on this PR ? i see that more bugs of this category are being filed - https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/558

comments.author: dmontagu
sandys
comments.body:
@dmontagu your PR on the deep copy was having trouble on 3.6. Also - do you believe from a performance perspective deepcopy is less performant than reinstantiation ?

@tiangolo ?

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys I noticed the problem with 3.6 but didn’t want to invest more in fixing it without some more feedback. (I’m not sure the changes are likely to be accepted yet even if I did work through the 3.6 issues.)

It’s not about performance — this happens once during app initialization, and will be insignificant from a performance perspective.

The thing that I find awkward about the code as it exists now is that the route is reinstantiated when calling include_router, potentially using a different class than the one that was used to create it in the first place. From a design perspective this feels a little awkward to me — the idea of “including” the router really seems to me more about the path (and possibly the extra dependencies and openapi spec considerations like tags). I feel like the class of the router (and any associated custom initialization of the route) should ideally not be repeated.

I can see multiple ways in which this design is convenient (doing the refactor wasn’t totally straightforward), but it also makes it a little more awkward to extend in creative ways.

issues.title: Do not want to include None attributes to JSON.
issues.body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Not related to a problem.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
I do not want to include None attributes to JSON result. FastAPI has an include_none option on jsonable_encoder method. However, there is no way to set this option False. 

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
Manually uses jsonable_encoder with include_none=True.

**Additional context**
If FastAPI provides a way to globally configure json encoder. It would be handy.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: For what it’s worth, I believe pydantic 1.0 is aiming to support custom json encoders per model. That may go a long way toward powering this feature (though may still require some changes to fastapi).

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
@daigok you can also use:

`response_model_include=None`
`response_model_exclude=set()`
`response_model_skip_defaults=None`
`response_model_exclude_unset`

in the path operation decorator, like:

```Python
@app.get("/", response_model_skip_defaults=None)
def read():
    ...
```

<!-- issue-manager: answered -->

issues.title: Improper error handling for lifespan protocol
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**
FastAPI makes use of the Lifespan protocol internally while starting up. According to the ASGI spec, if the application throws while handling a lifespan message, the server must understand it as the application not supporting that protocol, and errors should instead be signalled using `lifespan.startup.error` (I don't have the spec open, it might actually be called something else). FastAPI fails to do this, which results in strange error messages and potentially incorrect initialisation based on how the server is configured to react.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a file with the FastAPI hello world example
2. Launch the server on a port that is already being used by another process (or launch the server twice on the same port) using `uvicorn hello:app --host=0.0.0.0 --port=8000`
3. Port collision should cause socket binding to throw, see error

The uvicorn server will log that "The lifespan protocol appears unsupported". Depending on the error, and the server configuration, the application may be left running but the server will stop sending lifespan events as a result, so the shutdown event handlers(and possibly any user-defined startup handlers) will not be triggered either.

**Expected behavior**
FastAPI should correctly notify the server that an error was encountered while handling the lifespan event, and the server should exit after logging a lifespan error.

**Environment:**
 - OS: Windows
 - FastAPI Version: 0.38.1
- Python version: 3.6

**Additional context**
I know Uvicorn will sometimes leave applications running even if the lifespan handler raised an exception (which has happenned to me before and the ASGI spec says servers should allow) but I can't find a way to trigger this right now.

comments.author: sm-Fifteen
comments.body: I might actually be mixing up  encode/starlette#488, which was recently fixed and caused a similar error to occur, with an unrelated uvicorn issue that causes it to report the same error in case of port collision regardless of the application.

async def app(scope, receive, send):
    if scope['type'] == 'lifespan':
        while True:
            message = await receive()
            if message['type'] == 'lifespan.startup':
                # Do some startup here!
                await send({'type': 'lifespan.startup.complete'})
            elif message['type'] == 'lifespan.shutdown':
                # Do some shutdown here!
                await send({'type': 'lifespan.shutdown.complete'})
                return
    else:
        pass # Handle other types

issues.title: msgpack response if client asks for application/x-msgpack
issues.body:
hi,
we are in the process of evaling fastapi for a large app migration from flask. Quick question on a very interesting and useful piece of functionality. 
This builds upon the validations you have created for encoding json data and will be very useful for us out-of-the-box.

https://msgpack.org/index.html is an enhancement to json which is highly compressed. There are some benchmarks that show it is as fast as protocol buffers...while making it a drop-in alternative to json. 

msgpack is very important for Asia which are highly internet bandwidth constrained. We are prepared to tradeoff speed in exchange for lesser bandwidth (e.g. http://indiegamr.com/cut-your-data-exchange-traffic-by-up-to-50-with-one-line-of-code-msgpack-vs-json/ )

To be fair, it may be same size as gzipped json responses...but after having done android app deployments over a million devices in the field in asia, we realized that one has to write custom compression/decompression for gzipped json response handling. However msgpack has fairly well supported libraries. 

msgpack is fairly production ready. Lots of downstream python libraries use it in production - https://github.com/0rpc/zerorpc-python

This enhancement request is for fastapi to respond in msgpack if the requesting client ask for the mimetype `application/x-msgpack`. The default behavior continues to be the same as currently.

There is no impact to any client of a fastapi deployment unless they specifically ask for msgpack (in which case, they will get better performance).

I'm also pasting some criticisms of msgpack and comparisons with competing/derived json formats like CBOR

- https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=14067747
- http://zderadicka.eu/comparison-of-json-like-serializations-json-vs-ubjson-vs-messagepack-vs-cbor/
- https://techtutorialsx.com/2019/06/20/esp32-arduinojson-messagepack-serialization/
- https://gist.github.com/frsyuki/2908191
- https://prataprc.github.io/msgpack-vs-cbor.html

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys 
I think fastapi may actually be relatively well suited to handle this now without modifications.

If your top priority is just reducing bandwidth usage, I think it should be straightforward to implement converting the json responses to msgpack (in the presence of the appropriate mimetype request) as an ASGI middleware. I imagine that would involve parsing the json and dumping it to msgpack format, so would come with some processing overhead. But that overhead would probably be negligible compared to network, especially if bandwidth is the source of the concern.

If you really want to avoid the reparsing, I think this would also be possible by creating a custom starlette response that holds the python dict you want to serialize (rather than encoded json), and adding a middleware that converts responses of that type to a new starlette.Response with msgpack-encoded contents.

If you are serious about this and want/need a little more guidance than provided above, I can put together more of a skeleton implementation.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
This might be even easier than I thought.

I'd suggest taking a look at `starlette.responses.JSONResponse` -- it looks like you could pretty easily create a `MsgpackResponse` by defining an appropriate `render` function (e.g., using `msgpack-python`). Then, you could have your endpoints return `msgpack`-encoded data by setting `response_class=MsgpackResponse`. That wouldn't allow for dynamic determination of the response type based on the request, but maybe there is still a way to achieve that...

(Certainly, in the worst case, you could just perform a reparsing of the `JSONResponse` in a middleware.)

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys
Actually, I got interested in this and was able to throw together a working example -- not quite how I expected it would need to go, and it has some hacks that could probably be removed (maybe would require a feature request or two), but it works:

```python
from typing import Any, Dict, List, Type

import msgpack
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute, get_app
from starlette.background import BackgroundTask
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse, Response
from starlette.routing import request_response
from starlette.testclient import TestClient


class MsgpackResponse(Response):
    media_type = "application/msgpack"

    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)


class MultiResponse(JSONResponse):
    serialization_header = "Accept"
    default_response_class = JSONResponse
    response_class_map: Dict[str, Type[Response]] = {"application/x-msgpack": MsgpackResponse}

    def __init__(
        self,
        content: Any = None,
        status_code: int = 200,
        headers: dict = None,
        media_type: str = None,
        background: BackgroundTask = None,
    ) -> None:
        self.content = content
        self._status_code = status_code
        self._media_type = media_type
        self._background = background
        self.init_headers(headers)

    def for_request(self, request: Request) -> Response:
        serialization_method = request.headers.get(self.serialization_header)
        response_class = self.response_class_map.get(
            serialization_method, self.default_response_class
        )
        return response_class(
            content=self.content,
            status_code=self._status_code,
            headers=self.headers,
            media_type=self._media_type,
            background=self._background,
        )


class MultiAPIRoute(APIRoute):
    def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        if kwargs["response_class"] is JSONResponse:
            kwargs["response_class"] = MultiResponse
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        # Copied below from the end of the APIRoute init; overwriting the self.app with a modified version
        base_app_func = get_app(
            dependant=self.dependant,
            body_field=self.body_field,
            status_code=self.status_code,
            response_class=self.response_class,
            response_field=self.secure_cloned_response_field,
            response_model_include=self.response_model_include,
            response_model_exclude=self.response_model_exclude,
            response_model_by_alias=self.response_model_by_alias,
            response_model_skip_defaults=self.response_model_skip_defaults,
            dependency_overrides_provider=self.dependency_overrides_provider,
        )

        async def app_func(request: Request) -> Response:
            response = await base_app_func(request)
            if isinstance(response, MultiResponse):
                response = response.for_request(request)
            return response

        self.app = request_response(app_func)


app = FastAPI()
app.router.route_class = MultiAPIRoute


@app.get("/", response_model=List[int])
async def root():
    return [1, 2, 3]


client = TestClient(app)
print(client.get("/").content)
# b'[1,2,3]'

print(client.get("/", headers={"X-Serialization": "msgpack"}).content)
# b'\x93\x01\x02\x03'
```

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys 

I've reworked it to be substantially simpler, and also I've put some thought into what first-class support could/should look like in FastAPI.

-----

Here's a msgpack-specific simplified implementation (with minimal docs) that runs as is to show how simple it is:
(note `include_both_serialization_routes` could be removed with a relatively minor feature request, described below).
<details>
    <summary>Click to expand</summary>

```python
    from typing import Any, List, Tuple

    import msgpack
    from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
    from fastapi.routing import APIRoute
    from starlette.requests import Request
    from starlette.responses import JSONResponse
    from starlette.routing import Match
    from starlette.testclient import TestClient
    from starlette.types import Scope

    class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
        media_type = "application/x-msgpack"

        def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
            return msgpack.packb(content)

    class MsgpackRoute(APIRoute):
        detection_header = "Accept"
        detection_header_match = "appliaction/x-msgpack"
        response_class_mapping = {JSONResponse: MsgpackResponse}
        include_in_schema_override = False

        def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
            include_in_schema = kwargs["include_in_schema"] if self.include_in_schema_override is None else None
            response_class = self.response_class_mapping.get(kwargs["response_class"], kwargs["response_class"])
            kwargs["include_in_schema"] = include_in_schema
            kwargs["response_class"] = response_class
            super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        def matches(self, scope: Scope) -> Tuple[Match, Scope]:
            if not Request(scope).headers.get(self.detection_header) == self.detection_header_match:
                return Match.NONE, {}
            return super().matches(scope)

    def include_both_serialization_routes(
        parent_router: APIRouter, include_router: APIRouter
    ) -> None:
        original_route_class = parent_router.route_class
        parent_router.route_class = MsgpackRoute
        parent_router.include_router(include_router)
        parent_router.route_class = original_route_class
        parent_router.include_router(include_router)

    router = APIRouter()
    @router.get("/", response_model=List[int])
    async def root():
        return [1, 2, 3]

    app = FastAPI()
    include_both_serialization_routes(app.router, router)
    client = TestClient(app)

    print(client.get("/", headers={"Accept": "application/x-msgpack"}).content)
    # b'\x93\x01\x02\x03'

    print(client.get("/").content)
    # b'[1,2,3]'
```
</details>

-----

Next, here's a more general implementation that might be worthy of first-class support (**note:** it should run as is if you copy/paste):
<details>
    <summary>Click to expand</summary>

```python
    from typing import Any, List, Optional, ClassVar, get_type_hints, Tuple, Type
    
    import msgpack
    from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
    from fastapi.routing import APIRoute
    from starlette.requests import Request
    from starlette.responses import JSONResponse
    from starlette.routing import Match
    from starlette.testclient import TestClient
    from starlette.types import Scope
    
    class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
        media_type = "application/x-msgpack"
    
        def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
            return msgpack.packb(content)
    
    class OverrideResponseClassRoute(APIRoute):
        detection_header: ClassVar[str]
        detection_header_match: ClassVar[str]
        response_class_mapping: ClassVar[JSONResponse]
        include_in_schema_override: ClassVar[Optional[bool]] = None
    
        def __init_subclass__(cls) -> None:
            missing_attributes: List[str] = []
            for attribute in get_type_hints(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
                if not hasattr(cls, attribute):
                    missing_attributes.append(attribute)
            if missing_attributes:
                raise TypeError(f"{cls.__name__} did not define the following attributes: {missing_attributes}")
    
        def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
            include_in_schema = kwargs["include_in_schema"] if self.include_in_schema_override is None else None
            response_class = self.response_class_mapping.get(kwargs["response_class"], kwargs["response_class"])
    
            kwargs["include_in_schema"] = include_in_schema
            kwargs["response_class"] = response_class
    
            super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
    
        def matches(self, scope: Scope) -> Tuple[Match, Scope]:
            if not Request(scope).headers.get(self.detection_header) == self.detection_header_match:
                return Match.NONE, {}
            return super().matches(scope)
    
    class MsgpackRoute(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
        detection_header = "Accept"
        detection_header_match = "application/x-msgpack"
        response_class_mapping = {JSONResponse: MsgpackResponse}
        include_in_schema_override = False
    
    def include_typed_router(
        parent_router: APIRouter, include_router: APIRouter, *, route_class: Optional[Type[APIRoute]] = None
    ) -> None:
        original_route_class = parent_router.route_class
        parent_router.route_class = route_class or original_route_class
        try:
            parent_router.include_router(include_router)
        finally:
            parent_router.route_class = original_route_class
    
    router = APIRouter()
    @router.get("/", response_model=List[int])
    async def root():
        return [1, 2, 3]

    app = FastAPI()
    include_typed_router(app.router, router, route_class=MsgpackRoute)
    include_typed_router(app.router, router)
    client = TestClient(app)
    
    print(client.get("/", headers={"Accept": "application/x-msgpack"}).content)
    # b'\x93\x01\x02\x03'
    
    print(client.get("/").content)
    # b'[1,2,3]'
```
</details>

-----

Discussion of appropriate feature requests
-----

```python
class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
    media_type = "application/x-msgpack"

    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)
```
Getting this "built in" would probably require the following two feature requests:
* starlette: Add this class as a Response (NOT as a subclass of JSONResponse)
* fastapi: Allow non-None response_model if using a non-subclass of JSONResponse

(I think it is unlikely that fastapi would add this directly, but I suppose it's possible.)

Given how short it is, I expect it may be better not to upstream it, and have to deal with the additional testing burden, etc.

-----

```python
class OverrideResponseClassRoute(APIRoute):
    detection_header: ClassVar[str]
    detection_header_match: ClassVar[str]
    response_class_mapping: ClassVar[JSONResponse]
    include_in_schema_override: ClassVar[Optional[bool]] = None

    def __init_subclass__(cls) -> None:
        missing_attributes: List[str] = []
        for attribute in get_type_hints(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
            if not hasattr(cls, attribute):
                missing_attributes.append(attribute)
        if missing_attributes:
            raise TypeError(f"{cls.__name__} did not define the following attributes: {missing_attributes}")

    def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        """
        This could be made more typesafe by repeating all the args/kwargs explicitly.
        """
        include_in_schema = kwargs["include_in_schema"] if self.include_in_schema_override is None else None
        response_class = self.response_class_mapping.get(kwargs["response_class"], kwargs["response_class"])

        kwargs["include_in_schema"] = include_in_schema
        kwargs["response_class"] = response_class

        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def matches(self, scope: Scope) -> Tuple[Match, Scope]:
        if not Request(scope).headers.get(self.detection_header) == self.detection_header_match:
            return Match.NONE, {}
        return super().matches(scope)

class MsgpackRoute(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
    detection_header = "Accept"
    detection_header_match = "application/x-msgpack"
    response_class_mapping = {JSONResponse: MsgpackResponse}
    include_in_schema_override = False
```
@sandys is this what you had in mind for having the client specify the mimetype? One might want to parse the accept header into a list of strings, but this works for now.

-----

```python
def include_typed_router(
    parent_router: APIRouter, include_router: APIRouter, *, route_class: Optional[Type[APIRoute]] = None
) -> None:
    original_route_class = parent_router.route_class
    parent_router.route_class = route_class or original_route_class
    try:
        parent_router.include_router(include_router)
    finally:
        parent_router.route_class = original_route_class
```
This function could be removed if fastapi supported `route_class` parameter in APIRouter.include_router. I personally think this is a reasonable feature request that should be added.

-----

With the above feature requests, even without built-in support for msgpack, the ultimate usage would be as simple as:
```python
# ###################
# ##### msgpack #####
# ###################
class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
    media_type = "application/msgpack"
    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)

class MsgpackRoute(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
    detection_header = "Accept"
    detection_header_match = "application/x-msgpack"
    response_class_mapping = {JSONResponse: MsgpackResponse}
    include_in_schema_override = False
# ###################

router = APIRouter()
@router.get("/", response_model=List[int])
async def root():
    return [1, 2, 3]

app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(router, route_class=MsgpackRoute)
app.include_router(router)
```
@sandys Would this be good enough for you to consider it as being supported in a "first class" way? If not, is there anything specific you don't like, or think is missing?

Edit: I suppose it would be good to add support for receiving msgpack payloads as well; this would probably need to be done in a middleware.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
Rereading your original comment, I think I see more explicitly what you are looking for:

> This enhancement request is for fastapi to respond in msgpack if the requesting client ask for the mimetype application/x-msgpack.

I didn't think about the idea that this would just be handled by default for all apps. I think this could be done inside FastAPI using an approach similar to that proposed above, and could be an option to enable if msgpack is present. But I don't know if it's worth the additional maintenance burden on fastapi; I don't know how commonly used this serialization format is (especially compared to JSON).

@tiangolo If I had to guess, I would assume that this feature request is outside the scope of what you'd want to support out of the box. But if that's wrong, let me know and I can take a stab at a PR incorporating this directly into FastAPI.

comments.author: sandys
comments.body:
@dmontagu i would like to again add a million thanks to the work you have done above. I do not think i would have been able to figure this stuff out without putting weeks into it...and maybe still not (or would have given up).

And that is a good context to why this is important. I come from the Rails world and I frequently read about how big sites use Rails (e.g. [here](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=20920555)) . The key to observe here is "batteries included" but not linked to the batteries.

For example in Rails you can skip all the "batteries included" functionality by opt-out. Rails is opt-in by default ...and that is what im asking Fastapi devs to consider. 

`rails new app_name --skip-action-cable --skip-action-text `

Fastapi is quickly becoming more than just a wrapper on top of starlette. And that is a tradeoff between functionality versus simplicity (and even performance). For example Starlette still beats Fastapi on performance, but for a developer, there is no question which he will choose. It may come as no surprise that most people start with https://github.com/tiangolo/full-stack-fastapi-postgresql . I personally would not have been able to begin with Fastapi if i didnt have that starter readymade. 

 I think (and I really hope) that Fastapi keeps adding batteries , which are skippable. Because i know for sure - that without you writing the code above, there was an almost zero chance that I would have achieved it myself in time.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys I ended up having to go back to the older implementation to get it to work. I did have to make use of a private API on the request to replace its body (to prepare it to be consumed by pydantic). This could be handled without the override of the body, but it would involve a small change to a very long method in fastapi, somewhere near this line:
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/580cf8f4e2aac3d4f298fbb3ca1426f9ea6265de/fastapi/routing.py#L93

I opted for the small change to a short(er) method instead.

```python
import json
from typing import Any, Optional, Type, Dict

import msgpack
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse, Response
from starlette.routing import request_response
from starlette.testclient import TestClient

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute, get_app


class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
    media_type = "application/x-msgpack"

    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)


class MsgpackRoute(APIRoute):
    def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        original_app = get_app(
            dependant=self.dependant,
            body_field=self.body_field,
            status_code=self.status_code,
            response_class=self.response_class,
            response_field=self.secure_cloned_response_field,
            response_model_include=self.response_model_include,
            response_model_exclude=self.response_model_exclude,
            response_model_by_alias=self.response_model_by_alias,
            response_model_skip_defaults=self.response_model_skip_defaults,
            dependency_overrides_provider=self.dependency_overrides_provider,
        )

        async def app(request: Request) -> Response:
            if request.headers.get("Content-Type") == "application/x-msgpack":
                body = await request.body()
                request._body = json.dumps(jsonable_encoder(msgpack.loads(body))).encode()
            response = await original_app(request)
            if request.headers.get("Accept") == "application/x-msgpack":
                return MsgpackResponse(content=json.loads(response.body))
            return response

        self.app = request_response(app)


def include_typed_router(
    parent_router: APIRouter, include_router: APIRouter, *, route_class: Optional[Type[APIRoute]] = None
) -> None:
    original_route_class = parent_router.route_class
    parent_router.route_class = route_class or original_route_class
    try:
        parent_router.include_router(include_router)
    finally:
        parent_router.route_class = original_route_class


router = APIRouter()


@router.post("/")
async def root(body: Dict[str, Any]):
    return body


app = FastAPI()
include_typed_router(app.router, router, route_class=MsgpackRoute)
client = TestClient(app)

input = {"a": [1, 2, 3], "b": "c"}
msgpack_out_response = client.post("/", json=input, headers={"Accept": "application/x-msgpack"}).content
print(msgpack_out_response)
# b'\x82\xa1a\x93\x01\x02\x03\xa1b\xa1c'
assert jsonable_encoder(msgpack.loads(msgpack_out_response)) == input

msgpack_in_response = client.post(
    "/", data=msgpack_out_response, headers={"Content-Type": "application/x-msgpack"}
).content
print(msgpack_in_response)
# b'{"a":[1,2,3],"b":"c"}'
assert json.loads(msgpack_in_response) == input
```

This approach has the benefit of not adding extra routes to be matched.

Let me know if this implementation works for you.

-----

Going through this actually also revealed to me another good feature request for FastAPI: the ability to easily override the way the `self.app` is generated in the route class. This would make it easier to add header-specific route behavior by using a subclass of APIRoute. I'll throw together some PRs that would reduce the amount of custom code involved, but even if nothing is accepted the above example should still work for you.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys Yes, that is what I meant. The docs won't always show everything that is possible, but you shouldn't let that discourage you from trying to adapt the examples to more complex scenarios.

<details>
    <summary>Click to expand</summary>

```python
import json
from typing import Callable, List, Any, Awaitable

import msgpack
from starlette.testclient import TestClient
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import Response, JSONResponse

from fastapi import Body, FastAPI
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute

HandlerType = Callable[[Request], Awaitable[Response]]
MSGPACK_LABEL = "application/x-msgpack"


class MsgpackRequest(Request):
    async def body(self) -> bytes:
        if not hasattr(self, "_body"):
            body = await super().body()
            if MSGPACK_LABEL in self.headers.getlist("Content-Type"):
                body = json.dumps(jsonable_encoder(msgpack.loads(body))).encode()
            self._body = body
        return self._body


class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
    media_type = MSGPACK_LABEL

    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)

    @classmethod
    def from_response(cls, response: Response) -> "MsgpackResponse":
        content = json.loads(response.body)
        msgpack_response = MsgpackResponse(content, response.status_code, background=response.background)
        headers = response.headers
        for key, value in msgpack_response.headers.items():  # reset content-type and content-length
            headers[key] = value
        msgpack_response._headers = headers
        return msgpack_response


def msgpack_route_handler(handler: HandlerType) -> HandlerType:
    """
    Implemented as a decorator for easier composition; could be combined with gzip, for example
    """

    async def msgpack_handler(request: Request) -> Response:
        request = MsgpackRequest(request.scope, request.receive)
        response = await handler(request)
        if MSGPACK_LABEL in request.headers.getlist("Accept"):
            return MsgpackResponse.from_response(response)
        return response

    return msgpack_handler


class MsgpackRoute(APIRoute):
    def get_route_handler(self) -> Callable:
        route_handler = super().get_route_handler()
        route_handler = msgpack_route_handler(route_handler)
        return route_handler


app = FastAPI()
app.router.route_class = MsgpackRoute


@app.post("/sum")
async def sum_numbers(numbers: List[int] = Body(...)):
    return {"sum": sum(numbers)}

# Test that it works:

client = TestClient(app)


def msgpack_request(use_msgpack: bool, accept_msgpack: bool):
    n = 5
    headers = {}
    body = [1] * n
    if use_msgpack:
        data = msgpack.packb(body)
        headers["Content-Type"] = MSGPACK_LABEL
    else:
        data = json.dumps(body).encode()
    if accept_msgpack:
        headers["Accept"] = MSGPACK_LABEL
    response = client.post("/sum", data=data, headers=headers)
    if accept_msgpack:
        assert response.headers["Content-Type"] == MSGPACK_LABEL
        response_json = msgpack.loads(response.content, raw=False)
    else:
        response_json = response.json()
    assert response_json == {"sum": n}
    print(f"{data} -> {response.content}")


def confirm_it_works():
    for use_msgpack in [True, False]:
        for accept_msgpack in [True, False]:
            msgpack_request(use_msgpack=use_msgpack, accept_msgpack=accept_msgpack)
    print("Success")


confirm_it_works()

"""
b'\x95\x01\x01\x01\x01\x01' -> b'\x81\xa3sum\x05'
b'\x95\x01\x01\x01\x01\x01' -> b'{"sum":5}'
b'[1, 1, 1, 1, 1]' -> b'\x81\xa3sum\x05'
b'[1, 1, 1, 1, 1]' -> b'{"sum":5}'
Success
"""
```
</details>

This change basically allows you to use a handler-specific "middleware" that interacts with the request and response. (Note that the above approach can have issues with composing the requests, since only the `body()` logic of the last request is maintained.)

comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body:
Not sure about the current state about a potential `MsgPackResponse` class here, but my thinking goes that adding it to Starlette rather than FastAPI would make it more widely available, since many applications and frameworks (FastAPI) depend on Starlette.

In fact, looking at the app-wrapping logic in some of the snippets here, an ASGI `MsgPackMiddleware` shipped with Starlette (similar to [`GZipMiddleware`](https://www.starlette.io/middleware/#gzipmiddleware)) would definitely be even more appropriate and benefit the wider community even more, as it would be usable with any ASGI application.

(In general, I think any solution that embraces ASGI as the common compatibility interface should be considered first, rather than framework-specific solutions.)

comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body: Reading more about this issue and others, it seems there has been a variety of discussions around parsing the request body in various formats (gzip, msgpack, others), which Starlette currently does seem to support, and which isn't an ASGI-level piece of functionality. So my previous comment about an ASGI middleware *might* be misguided.

comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body:
> So my previous comment about an ASGI middleware *might* be misguided.

Actually, it was not. ;-)

I pushed [msgpack-asgi](https://github.com/florimondmanca/msgpack-asgi), an ASGI middleware that provides drop-in MessagePack support to/from JSON.

FastAPI example based on the one in the issue description (using HTTPX is not mandatory, I just happen to have it installed locally instead of Requests):

```python
import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI
from msgpack_asgi.middleware import MessagePackMiddleware

app = FastAPI()
app.add_middleware(MessagePackMiddleware)


@app.get("/")
async def root():
    return [1, 2, 3]


with httpx.Client(app=app, base_url="http://testserver") as client:
    print(client.get("/").content)
    # b'[1,2,3]'

    print(client.get("/", headers={"accept": "application/x-msgpack"}).content)
    # b'\x93\x01\x02\x03'
```

Should also work with Starlette and any other ASGI apps!

It also has an undocumented `MessagePackResponse` response class for cases when we'd want to explicitly send MessagePack data, though I'm not sure whether it is actually useful compared to the middleware.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
This is awesome!

It looks like you've handled most of the typical concerns with having middleware access the request body (e.g., not encoding a streaming response).

I'm wondering if maybe there is a way to encapsulate most/all of those checks into some kind of `BodyMiddleware` base class that has hooks for reading and/or updating the request and  response bodies. That might reduce the need for the `get_route_handler` method on `APIRoute`, as the main reason for its introduction was to fill the gap in access to the request/response bodies in traditional middleware.

Adding the `get_route_handler` was my own suggestion/contribution (with lots of input/help from @tiangolo of course), but it does feel to me like more of a workaround than a first class solution. If your approach to handling the bodies in middleware is more consistent with the rest of the ASGI ecosystem, it might make sense to stop recommending the use of `get_route_handler`.

------

Also related, I'm wondering if it could make sense to make it easier to add middlewares to specific routes and/or routers.

If the `get_route_handler` was replaced or augmented with a `get_app` function that applies the `request_response` to the result of `get_route_handler` *along* with any desired middleware, that could serve as the foundation for the ability to apply specific middlewares only to specific routes or routers, rather than the whole application, which seems like a relatively common request.

Alternatively, if it were easier to merge the openapi schemas of mounted subapps into the parent app, then I think selective middleware could be managed just through the use of different `FastAPI` instances when you want different middlewares.

comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body:
> This is awesome!

Cheers!

> It looks like you've handled most of the typical concerns with having middleware access the request body (e.g., not encoding a streaming response).

Well, not really, since currently `msgpack-asgi` does not support streaming responses (it raises a `NotImplementedError`). The main reason is that I'm not too sure what the semantics of a "streaming MessagePack response" would be, honestly. (It might make sense to stream individual MessagePack objects, in which case [Unpacker](https://github.com/msgpack/msgpack-python#streaming-unpacking) could be useful, but I don't even think that's a common pattern.)

(This makes me realize that `MessagePackMiddleware` currently consumes the full request body in order to replace it with the JSON-re-encoded version. I should definitely impose the same restriction than on responses, because malicious clients could keep sending more data and fill the RAM very easily. Edit: fixed in https://github.com/florimondmanca/msgpack-asgi/commit/b1fe8fbcba14d973cfc4f337251b942660ca8639)

So yeah, there are still some rough edges around streaming. :-) (and these might be unavoidable on the request side, as @tomchristie suggested in https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/495#issuecomment-494008175).

> I'm wondering if maybe there is a way to encapsulate most/all of those checks into some kind of `BodyMiddleware` base class

I think a simple form of this that consumes the request body and applies a transform function on it before passing it on is probably possible, yes, though it's probably not going to be optimal in most cases.

Here's a working implementation, though:

```python
import functools
import typing

from starlette.datastructures import Headers
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Send, Scope, Message

BodyTransformer = typing.Callable[[bytes, Headers], bytes]


class RequestBodyMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, transform: BodyTransformer):
        self.app = app
        self.transform = transform

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        if scope["type"] != "http":
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        app = RequestBodyResponder(self.app, transform=self.transform)
        await app(scope, receive, send)


class RequestBodyResponder:
    def __init__(
        self, app: ASGIApp, transform: BodyTransformer = lambda body, _: body,
    ):
        self.app = app
        self.transform = transform
        self.initial_message: Message = {}
        self.headers = Headers()

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        assert scope["type"] == "http"
        self.headers = Headers(scope=scope)
        receive = functools.partial(self._receive, receive)
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)

    async def _receive(self, receive: Receive) -> Message:
        body = b""
        more_body = True
        while more_body:
            message = await receive()
            assert message["type"] == "http.request"
            body += message["body"]
            more_body = message.get("more_body", False)

        message["body"] = self.transform(body, self.headers)

        return message
```

Example usage for the GZip request body use case documented [here](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/custom-request-and-route/#handling-custom-request-body-encodings):

```python
import gzip

from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.datastructures import Headers
from pydantic import BaseModel

from .request_body_middleware import RequestBodyMiddleware

app = FastAPI()


def gzip_decompress(body: bytes, headers: Headers) -> bytes:
    if "gzip" in headers.getlist("Content-Encoding"):
        return gzip.decompress(body)
    return body


app.add_middleware(RequestBodyMiddleware, transform=gzip_decompress)


class Body(BaseModel):
    message: str


@app.post("/")
async def root(body: Body):
    return {"body": body}
```

Test with:

```bash
echo '{"message": "test"}' | gzip | curl -i http://localhost:8000 -H 'Content-Encoding: gzip' --data-binary @-
```

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
date: Fri, 01 Nov 2019 20:13:18 GMT
server: uvicorn
content-length: 27
content-type: application/json

{"body":{"message":"test"}}
```

I wrote earlier "the implementation is not going to be optimal in most cases". In the GZip case, it is not optimal because the middleware is going to load the whole request body into memory before passing it to the application — regardless of whether the application uses `await request.body()` or `await request.stream()`. We could implement a more subtle solution that maintains a `BytesIO` buffer and a `GZipFile` (much like what `GZipMiddleware` does, but in reverse), but that would probably mean adding extra hooks and APIs that more simple use cases won't need.

In the end, building a performant specialized middleware is probably better than having a potentially sloppy generic middleware. :-)

(Also, I only mentioned processing requests here because building a generic middleware that *also* supports transforming the response is even more subtle. There are a few corner cases that need to be handled e.g. updating the `Content-Length`, having support for changing the `Content-Type`, and then the same kind of issues with handling a streaming body.)

---

@dmontagu If the APIs added to FastAPI to support modifying the request body mainly aimed at adding support for GZip request bodies (an issue also mentioned in https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/644), an alternative worth considering is working on extending the `GZipMiddleware` shipped with Starlette to also support decoding gzipped requests (with support for streaming etc. as hinted above).

comments.author: paolieri
comments.body:
@spacemanspiff2007 You can run a function in the same thread pool used by Starlette/FastAPI using [fastapi_utils.tasks.repeat_every](https://fastapi-utils.davidmontague.xyz/user-guide/repeated-tasks/) by @dmontagu.

With this `example.py`
``` python
import logging
import time
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi_utils.tasks import repeat_every

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
app = FastAPI()
counter = 0

@app.get('/')
def hello():
    return 'Hello'

@app.on_event("startup")
@repeat_every(seconds=1, logger=logger, wait_first=True)
def periodic():
    global counter
    print('counter is', counter)
    counter += 1
```
you get
```
$ uvicorn example:app
INFO:     Started server process [85016]
INFO:     Waiting for application startup.
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
counter is 0
counter is 1
counter is 2
counter is 3
```
The implementation is quite simple ([source here](https://github.com/dmontagu/fastapi-utils/blob/master/fastapi_utils/tasks.py)): it runs the given function periodically, using Starlette's `starlette.concurrency.run_in_threadpool`. This is precisely how Starlette and FastAPI run background tasks ([source here](https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/master/starlette/background.py)).

But, since this is the same thread pool used to serve requests (of endpoints defined with `def`), if your task is heavy you may want to run it in a separate process. For example,
- You could start a separate process with `subprocess.Popen` and periodically check its status from FastAPI's thread pool using `repeat_every` (this could become messy when you have many tasks to check upon);
- You could use a task queue like Celery or Arq, which run as a separate process (or many processes if you use multiple workers). Celery has [periodic tasks](https://docs.celeryproject.org/en/stable/userguide/periodic-tasks.html) but honestly, if all you want is to run a batch script, `crond` should do.

comments.body: @tiangolo I'd recommend to consider integrating the [Advanced Python Scheduler](https://github.com/agronholm/apscheduler). In my experience it offers a lot of features and is very stable/works very well. It offers a lot of [schedulers, job stores, executors and triggers](https://apscheduler.readthedocs.io/en/latest/userguide.html#choosing-the-right-scheduler-job-store-s-executor-s-and-trigger-s), has support for [event listeners](https://apscheduler.readthedocs.io/en/latest/userguide.html#scheduler-events) and is [extensible](https://apscheduler.readthedocs.io/en/latest/extending.html#extending-apscheduler). Consequently APScheduler is the go-to integration for flask [flask-apscheduler](https://github.com/viniciuschiele/flask-apscheduler) and django [django-apscheduler](https://github.com/jarekwg/django-apscheduler) as well. The test coverage of 94% is very good as well.

comments.author: cryptoroo
comments.body:
I agree with @fkromer 

I have bein using APScheduler for scheduled tasks. 

There are really good features such as being able to see when the next schedule runs, cancelling schedules without rebooting the FastAPI Process. 

I have been pinning certain endpoints to the schedule and fixing the IDs so it doesn't get scheduled twice.

This can be updated to be a query param or route.  

Also it would be worthwhile persisting the schedules in some way as every time the FastAPI service restarts it doesn't automatically pick it up. 

Here is some basic boostrap code. 

**Create Schedule Object:**

```
from apscheduler.schedulers.asyncio import AsyncIOScheduler

Schedule = AsyncIOScheduler()
Schedule.start()

```

**View All Scheduled Jobs:** 

```
@app.get("/schedule/show_schedules/",tags=["schedule"])
async def get_scheduled_syncs():
    schedules = []
    for job in Schedule.get_jobs():
        schedules.append({"Name": str(job.id), "Run Frequency": str(job.trigger), "Next Run": str(job.next_run_time)})
    return schedules
```

**Add A Scheduled Job**

```
@app.post("/schedule/schedule1/",tags=["schedule"])
async def add_chat_job_to_scheduler(time_in_seconds:int=300):
    schedule1_job = Schedule.add_job(schedule1_function, 'interval', seconds=time_in_seconds,id="schedule1")
    return {"Scheduled":True,"JobID":schedule1_job.id}

@app.post("/schedule/schedule2/",tags=["schedule"])
async def add_schedule2(time_in_hours:int=24):
    schedule2_job = Schedule.add_job(schedule2_function,, 'interval', hours=time_in_hours,id="schedule2")
    return {"Scheduled":True,"JobID":schedule2_job.id}
```

**Delete a scheduled Job**

```
@app.delete("/schedule/schedule1/",tags=["schedule"])
async def remove_schedule1():
    Schedule.remove_job("schedule1")
    return {"Scheduled":False,"JobID":"schedule1"}

@app.delete("/schedule/schedule2/",tags=["schedule"])
async def remove_schedule2(time_in_hours:int=24):
    Schedule.remove_job("schedule2")
    return {"Scheduled":False,"JobID":"schedule2"}
```

comments.author: havardthom
comments.body:
I'm currently using Arq workers in my FastAPI app for scheduled jobs, based on https://github.com/samuelcolvin/arq/issues/182

Example code:

`example_main.py`
```
import logging
from fastapi import FastAPI

from example_scheduler import arq_worker

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
async def root():
    await arq_worker.worker.pool.enqueue_job("hello_world")
    return "Welcome to root"

@app.on_event("startup")
async def startup_event():
    await arq_worker.start(handle_signals=False)

@app.on_event("shutdown")
async def shutdown_event():
    await arq_worker.close()

def run_dev_server():
    logger.info("Running Uvicorn without Gunicorn and with reloading")
    import uvicorn

    app_module = "example_main:app"
    uvicorn.run(
        app_module,
        reload=True,
    )

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_dev_server()

```

`example_scheduler.py`
```
import logging
import asyncio
from arq.worker import create_worker
from arq.connections import RedisSettings
from arq import cron

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

async def hello_world(ctx):
    job_id = ctx["job_id"]
    print(f"hello world ({job_id})")

class ArqWorkerSettings:
    functions = [hello_world]
    # TODO: Issue with unique cron jobs and multiple arq workers: https://github.com/samuelcolvin/arq/issues/196
    cron_jobs = [
        cron(hello_world, second=1, unique=True)
    ]
    redis_settings = RedisSettings()

class ArqWorker:
    def __init__(self):
        self.worker = None
        self.task = None

    async def start(self, **kwargs):
        self.worker = create_worker(ArqWorkerSettings, **kwargs)
        self.task = asyncio.create_task(self.worker.async_run())

    async def close(self):
        await self.worker.close()

arq_worker = ArqWorker()

```

comments.author: lambdaq
comments.body: the reason periodic tasks are not supported by most web frameworks is because server process are spawned in a "stateless" way, so if one of your process spawns a scheduled task, how do you guarantee that other worker process didn't also start the same task? To de-duplicate and make sure tasks are fired only once, the cluster must be made aware of *all* worker's state.  This is far beyond the scope of conventional web framework. Thus I suggest we close this issue.

issues.title: Double-nested router can't have empty path(despite having prefix at grandparent level)
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**
If I have a router setup like this: `main router -> sub router -> sub sub router`. And I include `sub router` with a prefix, I can't have an empty path parameter on any routes in the `sub sub router`.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a file with double-nested routers:
```Python
from fastapi import APIRouter

sub_sub_router = APIRouter()
sub_router = APIRouter()
main_router = APIRouter()

main_router.include_router(sub_router, prefix='/foo')
sub_router.include_router(sub_sub_router)
```
2. Add a path operation function without a path:
```Python
@sub_sub_router.get('')
def test():
    return 'bar'
```
3. See error: `Exception: Prefix and path cannot be both empty (path operation: test)`

**Expected behavior**
Since at one point in the path there is a prefix, "test" should be served at /foo.

**Additional context**
This is probably a choice to make on whether you want to support this use-case. To explain why I do it like this: we have a pretty complex API, and to reduce complexity(and prevent files being thousands of lines long) each endpoint(i.e.: /users) has a folder structure like:

- users/
  - \_\_init\_\_.py <-- contains main users router, includes read & write.py's routers
  - read.py <-- contains a router with get/read actions for users
  - write.py <-- contains a router with write actions for users
- routes.py <-- includes the user router from users/\_\_init\_\_.py and sets the prefix to '/users'

So for my usecase I have 2 alternative ways to work around this issue:

- Include read/write directly in the routes.py(this removes some abstraction though, maybe at one point we'll split it even more than just read/write)
- Set the prefix in users/\_\_init\_\_.py to '/users'(this moves the route definitions to a bunch of different files which is kind of meh)

I'm perfectly fine with using one of these workarounds if I have to, but before I do I wanted to know if you see this behavior as a bug as well. I checked out pull request #415 to see if I could make a pull request that fixes this, but unfortunately I don't know how to reach the parent router in the code that was changed there.

teuneboon
